article.title: Antarctic Ice Cores Reveal Earth Is Traveling Through Ancient Supernova Dust, Tracing the Solar System's Journey Inside the Local Interstellar Cloud
article.summary: Iron-60 in 40,000- to 80,000-year-old Antarctic ice confirms our solar system is passing through a cloud of ancient stellar debris, with the sun expected to exit within a few thousand years.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A team of researchers led by nuclear astrophysicist Dr. Dominik Koll at Germany's Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf has detected iron-60 — a radioactive isotope forged exclusively in stellar explosions — in Antarctic ice cores dating between 40,000 and 80,000 years old, providing the most detailed chronicle yet of Earth's ongoing passage through the Local Interstellar Cloud. The findings, published on May 13, 2026 in [Physical Review Letters](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-cores-earth.html) (DOI: 10.1103/nxjq-jwgp), confirm that our solar system entered this vast cloud of ancient supernova debris somewhere between 40,000 and 124,000 years ago and is expected to exit within a few thousand years.

## What We Know

Iron-60 is essentially impossible to produce through any terrestrial or solar process. As [ScienceAlert](https://www.sciencealert.com/stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-earths-journey-through-the-cosmos) explains, the isotope has "a half-life of just 2.6 million years," meaning any iron-60 present when Earth formed 4.5 billion years ago has long since decayed. Any iron-60 found on Earth today must therefore have arrived recently from space — specifically, condensed into stardust particles from a supernova explosion and carried across interstellar distances.

"We found supernova-produced 60Fe in Antarctic ice," Koll said, as reported by [Space.com](https://www.space.com/science/scientists-found-stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-what-could-it-tell-us-about-our-solar-system). "If there is 60Fe condensed into dust particles (stardust) then this dust can penetrate the shielding of the solar system and end up on Earth."

The research team processed approximately 300 kilograms of ice transported from the Alfred Wegener Institute Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research (AWI), which maintains one of the world's most extensive ice core archives, according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-cores-earth.html). The ice samples were drawn from cores collected by the European Project for Ice Coring in Antarctica (EPICA), as [ScienceAlert](https://www.sciencealert.com/stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-earths-journey-through-the-cosmos) notes. Despite the large starting mass, the extraction process yielded only a few hundred milligrams of dust, according to [Astrobiology.com](https://astrobiology.com/2026/05/astrochemistry-surrounded-by-stardust-iron-60-discovery-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-local-interstellar-cloud-leaves-its-mark.html). Koll described the detection challenge as being "like searching for a needle in 50,000 football stadiums filled to the roof with hay."

To isolate iron-60 atoms from the dust, the team used accelerator mass spectrometry at the Heavy Ion Accelerator Facility (HIAF) at Australian National University, a technique capable of identifying individual isotopes among trillions of competing atoms. The researchers also processed 500 kilograms of recent Antarctic snow to establish a modern baseline, according to [EarthSky](https://earthsky.org/space/stardust-in-antarctica-earth-crossed-supernova-cloud/), and used beryllium-10 and aluminium-26 as verification control isotopes, as described by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-cores-earth.html).

The key comparative finding is directional: "The concentration of iron-60 in ice from tens of thousands of years ago is significantly lower than the concentration in snow from the past few decades," [ScienceAlert](https://www.sciencealert.com/stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-earths-journey-through-the-cosmos) reports. In other words, the ancient ice contains less iron-60 than today's snow. As [EarthSky](https://earthsky.org/space/stardust-in-antarctica-earth-crossed-supernova-cloud/) explains, "the amount of iron-60 was sparser in the past than it is now," indicating the solar system has been moving progressively deeper into the cloud over the timeframe represented by the ice record.

"This result suggests that less interstellar dust was reaching Earth during that period," Koll told [Space.com](https://www.space.com/science/scientists-found-stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-what-could-it-tell-us-about-our-solar-system).

## The Local Interstellar Cloud

The Local Interstellar Cloud — sometimes called the Local Fluff — is one of roughly 15 individual interstellar clouds in the galaxy's immediate neighborhood, according to [Phys.org's Conversation piece](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-reveals-tens.html). Our solar system has been threading through it for somewhere between 40,000 and 124,000 years, per [Space.com's](https://www.space.com/science/scientists-found-stardust-trapped-in-antarctic-ice-what-could-it-tell-us-about-our-solar-system) reporting, and is currently near the cloud's edge, according to [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-cores-earth.html). The solar system is expected to exit the cloud within a few thousand years.

The presence of iron-60 in the cloud carries a significant implication for its origin. "This means that the clouds surrounding the Solar System are linked to a stellar explosion. And for the first time, this gives us the opportunity to investigate the origin of these clouds," Koll said, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260513221751.htm).

However, iron-60 levels are lower than what a direct supernova origin would predict, complicating the picture. As Koll noted in a piece published through [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-05-stardust-antarctic-ice-reveals-tens.html): "If these clouds did originate directly from an exploding star, we would expect way more iron-60 than we actually see." The finding points instead to a more complex history, possibly involving multiple stellar sources or significant dilution of the debris across time and space.

## What We Don't Know

The study does not identify the specific supernova or supernovae responsible for the iron-60 now falling on Earth. Previous research has linked a cluster of nearby stellar explosions over the past few million years — concentrated in a region sometimes called the Local Bubble — to shaping the broader interstellar environment around our solar system, but the precise provenance of the Local Interstellar Cloud's stellar debris remains open.

The ice record used in this study begins roughly 80,000 years ago, which may not extend far enough back to capture the full entry event into the cloud. To address this, the team is planning future analyses of older ice, in collaboration with the Beyond EPICA – Oldest Ice project, which aims to recover cores up to 1.5 million years old, according to [Astrobiology.com](https://astrobiology.com/2026/05/astrochemistry-surrounded-by-stardust-iron-60-discovery-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-local-interstellar-cloud-leaves-its-mark.html). The researchers hope that older cores will reveal the iron-60 signal before the solar system entered the cloud, providing a cleaner before-and-after comparison.

## Analysis

The use of Antarctic ice as a long-term cosmic detector is an established but continually refined technique. Previous studies have found iron-60 in deep-sea sediments and lunar regolith, but ice cores offer a time-resolved archive that sediment records cannot match at the same resolution. The EPICA cores used here, combined with the sensitivity of accelerator mass spectrometry, allowed the team to reconstruct a rough density profile of iron-60 influx across 80,000 years — effectively turning the ice sheet into a slow-motion particle detector aimed at the interstellar medium.

The result joins a growing body of work documenting how the solar system's path through the galaxy leaves measurable imprints on Earth's geochemical record. The Local Interstellar Cloud is the most immediate such environment, and the iron-60 signal in Antarctic ice now offers the first direct terrestrial evidence of its supernova-linked chemistry, co-authored by Koll alongside Prof. Anton Wallner and Annabel Rolofs of the University of Bonn, among others, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260513221751.htm) and [Astrobiology.com](https://astrobiology.com/2026/05/astrochemistry-surrounded-by-stardust-iron-60-discovery-in-antarctic-ice-reveals-local-interstellar-cloud-leaves-its-mark.html).
